_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Green County, WI. Records are excellent from county formation (1836). The Swiss colony at New Glarus generated distinctive records — Swiss German church registers, cheese factory patronage lists, and Emigration Society documents.

current_courthouse.location: Monroe, WI — 1016 16th Avenue (historic courthouse, still in use with additions)

previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by current Romanesque Revival building.

record_losses.cause: None — no major fire or war damage
record_losses.description: Green County has experienced no significant courthouse fire or record loss. Records are continuous from the territorial era. The Swiss colony records at New Glarus add a unique genealogical layer.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Green County's records are intact. If {ancestor_name} lived here, the paper trail survives. For Swiss families, the Emigration Society records at the Wisconsin Historical Society may document the original emigration from Canton Glarus.
record_losses.source: Wisconsin Historical Society. Guide to Wisconsin County Records.

deeds.repository: Green County Register of Deeds; Wisconsin Historical Society microfilm

wills_estates.repository: Green County Probate Court; Wisconsin Historical Society

marriages.repository: Green County Register of Deeds; Wisconsin Historical Society

court_minutes.repository: Green County Clerk of Courts; Wisconsin Historical Society

vital_records.completeness: Statewide registration from 1907. Pre-1907 rely on Swiss Reformed and Norwegian Lutheran church records.
vital_records.repository: Wisconsin Department of Health Services; Swiss colony church records at Wisconsin Historical Society

substitute_sources.description: The Emigration Society of Canton Glarus organized the 1845 colony. Records include passenger lists, financial accounts, and correspondence documenting the founding families.
substitute_sources.repository: Wisconsin Historical Society; New Glarus Historical Society; Landesarchiv Glarus (Switzerland)
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critical for tracing the original 108 colonists and subsequent Swiss arrivals. These records connect American families to their exact Swiss origins.
substitute_sources.source: Luchsinger, John. The Swiss Colony of New Glarus. Madison: State Historical Society of Wisconsin, 1879.

substitute_sources.description: The Swiss Reformed Church at New Glarus maintained records in Swiss German. Baptisms, confirmations, marriages, and burials documented the colony's families.
substitute_sources.repository: New Glarus Historical Society; Wisconsin Historical Society; individual congregation
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for Swiss families. Records in Swiss German until the early twentieth century. Cross-referencing with Canton Glarus parish records enables complete genealogical chains.
substitute_sources.source: Luchsinger, The Swiss Colony of New Glarus (1879).

substitute_sources.description: Green County's 100+ cheese factories maintained patronage lists, milk delivery records, and payment records. Where these survive, they document every dairy farmer in the factory's catchment area.
substitute_sources.repository: Green County Historical Society; Wisconsin Historical Society; individual factories or successors
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Cheese factory patronage lists document farm families between census years. Monthly payment records establish income levels and farm productivity.
substitute_sources.source: Apps, Jerry. Cheese: The Making of a Wisconsin Tradition. Amherst, WI: Amherst Press, 1998.

narrative_hooks: Green County's records are complete from the territorial era — and for Swiss families at New Glarus, the Emigration Society records may trace {ancestor_name} all the way back to Canton Glarus.
The Swiss Reformed Church at New Glarus kept records in Swiss German — {ancestor_name}'s baptism may be recorded in a language that the grandchildren could no longer read.

Cheese factory patronage lists in Green County document every dairy farmer who delivered milk — {ancestor_name} appears not in the courthouse but in the factory ledger, measured in pounds of milk and checks per month.
Wisconsin vital records begin in 1907 — for {ancestor_name} before that date, the Swiss or Norwegian church records are the primary source.
source_references: Wisconsin Historical Society. Guide to Wisconsin County Records.
Luchsinger, John. The Swiss Colony of New Glarus. Madison: State Historical Society of Wisconsin, 1879.
Apps, Jerry. Cheese: The Making of a Wisconsin Tradition. Amherst, WI: Amherst Press, 1998.
